free_text: Dependence of the prompt fission gamma-ray spectrum
on the entrance channel of compound nucleus:
Spontaneous vs. neutron-induced fission
DANCE array.
The prompt fission gamma-ray emission for
both fission channels of both compound nuclei was
measured using the DANCE array in coincidence with
the detection of fission fragments by a compact
parallel-plate avalanche counter (PPAC), designed
specifically for DANCE.
4 spectra of normalized to 1 counts (for convenience).
These 2 papers can serve as reference to analysis:
Physical Review C 87, 034620 (2013)
Physical Review C 90, 014602 (2014)
The errors are computed first at the raw
counts spectra as statistical uncertainties sqrt(n)
for both signal and background spectra. Then the
errors just propagate during further analysis.
NO further corrections should be made to these
spectra. What is being published is NOT the raw
counts spectra that we get from the detectors. What
is being published is what wauthors call unfolded
(true, emitted) spectra. Many procedures were
applied to the raw data, such as measuring,
normalizing, and removing backrounds, time sorting,
energy calibrations, etc. The last part of the
analysis was "unfolding", ie, moving from measured to
true spectra. Unfolding includes: building a GEANT4
model of all detectors, simulating with Monte Carlo
measured spectra, simulating a kernel for all
possible values of Eg in a fission reaction, building
an unfolding 2D and 4D matrices, and unfolding
measured spectra using an iterative Bayes method. The
reference for unfolding analysis: Physical Review C
90, 014602 (2014). The figure 3 shows the difference
between measured and unfolded Eg and Mg spectra, and
a 2D spectrum of Eg-Mg. Also a validation of the
unfolded data was done. We used unfolded spectra as
an input for the GEANT4 model, so the unfolded
spectrum serves as a source of the fission reaction.
Then this reaction is simulated, and the output is a
simulated spectrum, which is like a measured
spectrum. We compare this simulated spectrum to the
measured spectrum, they should be the same. If they
are not the same, then changes to the GEANT4 model of
detectors are made, and everything is repeated again.
So we iterate this whole process until no more
improvements are observed. The GEANT4 model produced
simulated data remarkably close to the measured one.
Approved by A.Chyzh on April 15, 2026.
BP
The Lujan Neutron Scattering Center
at LANL/LANSCE.
Neutrons were produced first by bombarding a
tungsten target with an 800 MeV proton beam at a
repetition rate of 20 Hz and then moderated by water.
For neutron-induced fission experiments, PPACs with
either 239Pu or 241Pu targets were assembled at LLNL
and bombarded by neutrons with energies from thermal
up to several hundred keV. A total of over 10**6
fission events with at least one gamma-ray detected by
DANCE were collected for both isotopes.
Fig. - 3a
Data were received from A.Chyzh on April 12, 2026.

Neutrons were produced first by bombarding a
tungsten target with an 800 MeV proton beam at a
repetition rate of 20 Hz and then moderated by water.
For neutron-induced fission experiments, PPACs with
either 239Pu or 241Pu targets were assembled at LLNL
and bombarded by neutrons with energies from thermal
up to several hundred keV. A total of over 10**6
fission events with at least one gamma-ray detected by
DANCE were collected for both isotopes.
Fig. - 3c
Data were received from A.Chyzh on April 12, 2026.
For the spontaneous fission, PPACs with a total mass
of about 642 microg of 242Pu enriched to 99.93% or
about 769 microg of 240Pu enriched to 98.86% were
assembled at LLNL and used for the fission-fragment
detection in coincidence with the detection of the
prompt gamma-rays by DANCE. A total of ~10**5 fission
events with at least one gamma-ray detected by DANCE
were collected for both targets.
Fig. - 3b
Data were received from A.Chyzh on April 12, 2026.
